RUTAN & TUCKER
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS

GARVIN F. SHALENBERGER    STAN WOLCOTT*              ELIZABETH L. HANNA        L. SKI HARRISON
JAMES H. RUTAN*           ROBERT E. TUCKER*         DAVID M. KIRCHNER        TRACY M. BLUMBERG
WILLIAM A. RUTAN*         MARCIA A. FORSYTH         RICHARD A. BRUMFIELD     JUDITH D. DROZ
WILLIAM B. RIEL           WILLIAM N. MORRISON       SCOTT A. PINZONE         DAVID M. REAVES
THOMAS J. TUCKER*         GARY A. RICHARDSON        JAMES L. GROVHOLED       KEVIN M. GREGORY
JOHN B. HURLBURT, JR      ANNE NELSON LANPHAR       LORI GARNER SMITH        BRIAN P. KAVASCH
JOHN K. LYON              DAVID B. HANSEN           CHARLES E. MORRISON      DAN SLATS
WILLIAM K. MOORE          MICHAEL T. BRADY          JAMES W. WHITE           ROBERT ROBERTS
DONALD E. ARRINGTON*      STEPHEN M. HYDE           KATHY FORBATH ESTAHAN    JAMES S. WETZ
EDWARD R. BOYLE           PHILIP D. KOHN            DAVID B. CORDROY         LINDA M. GOULD
JAMES A. RUTAN*           JOEL D. KOPPELMAN         THOMAS M. WALSH          DAVID T. WARD
ROGER A. OHASLI*          ROBERT M. MCCURRY         JAYNE TAYLOR KACER       DARWIN KINOSHTA
KENNETH L. FREEMAN, JR*   WILLIAM W. WYNEKEN       MICHAEL A. VICKERS       MICHAEL G. CRISS
GEORGE W. REEVES          EVRIDIKI TIGANI DALLAS   STEPHEN A. ELLIS         PATRICK S. MCCALLA
ROBERT W. ALBERTS         MARY M. GREEN            WILLIAM F. GAITHER       RICHARD K. NOVELL
MICHAEL D. BAKER          TIMOTHY J. BING          MICHAEL M. TURNER        MICHAEL S. LORD
LAWRENCE M. MELLEN        THOMAS T. BRUCE          RICHARD A. TEER          SHEILA R. VASQUEZ
IRVIN L. RIVA*            MARK S. FRAZIER          ADAM M. VOLKERT          RUSSELL A. RUTAN*
JEFFREY M. COLEMAN*       KARIN T. KNUDSEN         JEFFREY A. GOLDFARB      ELLEN S. KANCROFT
ROBERT SWANSON            MARGARET L. BARRSON      ROBERT N. BARROWS        DANIEL J. RHODES
                                                   STEPHEN B. OTTO          FRANK W. BATTAILE
                                                   GARY D. MCCABE           STEVE W. SLOAN
                                                   LAYNE M. WETZLER         HAVEN S. PETERSON
                                                                             PAUL R. SWANSON
                                                                             MELISSA G. BECCA

BANK OF THE WEST, SUITE 1400

611 ANTON BOULEVARD

COSTA MESA, CALIFORNIA 92626

DIRECT ALL MAIL TO: P. O. BOX 1950

COSTA MESA, CALIFORNIA 92626-1950

TELEPHONE (714) 641-5100
(213) 625-7585

TELECOPIER (714) 546-0228

                                                   *A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

J. A. W. RUTAN (1880-1972)                        DAVID J. GARIBALDI III
JAMES H. TUCKER, SR. (1888-1950)                  OF COUNSEL
WILFORD W. DAHL, SR. (1919-1943)
M. RODGER HOWELL (1904-1983)


                                        August 12, 1991


John Wallace, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807


Re: Request for Formal Written Advice on Behalf of The
    Irvine Transportation Authority

Dear Mr. Wallace:

     On behalf of the Irvine Transportation Authority (ITA),
I am requesting formal written advice pursuant to Government
Code Section 83114(b) and 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18329(b). As City Attorney for the City of Irvine, I
am the authorized representative of the ITA.

     At its September meeting to be held on September 17,
1991, the ITA will consider whether to recommend approval to
the City Council of an ordinance regarding the regulation of
traffic in Irvine. I would appreciate a response to my
request within 21 days, as required by Section 83114(b).

1.   Does the ITA Have "Decision-Making Authority"?

     The ITA was established in May 1989 to advise the City
Council on traffic issues. (Irvine Code of Ordinances,

John Wallace, Esq.
August 12, 1991
Page -2-


Section VI.F-501 et seq., copy enclosed.)  It is comprised of
11 members, including the 5 City Councilmembers and 6 other
members appointed by the City Council.  (Irvine Code, Section
VI.F-522.)  The City Code specifically states that the ITA
does not itself have the power to implement any city policies
or procedures; rather, the ITA makes recommendations to City
Council, which the Council may accept or reject.  In order
for an ITA decision to become operative, it must be adopted
by a City Council resolution or ordinance.  (Sections VI.F-
533 and 534.)

     The ITA meets only once a month.  It has not yet made a
recommendation to the City Council regarding the proposed
ordinance.

     Do the members of the ITA have disqualification and
disclosure duties under the Political Reform Act?  I believe
that the answer turns on whether the ITA is a "board or
commission with decision-making authority."  (2 Cal. Code of
Regs., Section 18700(a)(2).)  Because the ITA does not make,
compel or prevent final governmental decisions, and because
the City Council historically has not approved ITA's
recommendations without significant modification, I have
tentatively concluded that the ITA does not meet the
regulatory definition.  Do you agree?

     If the ITA is deemed not to be a "board or commission
with decision-making authority," must its members disqualify
themselves from decisions that would affect their financial
interests?  Must they file Statements of Economic Interests?

2.  Are ITA Members Covered by Regulation 18703?

     Five members of the ITA are nominated by various local
organizations (the University of California at Irvine, the
Industrial League of Orange County, the Irvine Chamber of
Commerce, the Irvine Business Complex Consortium and the
Building Industry of Southern California).  Within this group
of five members, the four members nominated by trade
associations typically do not work directly for the
association; rather, they are employed by a member company,
e.g., the member nominated by the Building Industry
Association works for a local builder, not the Association.
The membership of industry representatives on the ITA arises
from the City Council's desire to have input from all

Rutan & Tucker
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS


John Wallace, Esq.
August 12, 1991
Page -3-


segments of the Irvine community affected by traffic and
congestion. The City Council declared in the Ordinance
creating ITA that traffic issues affect "residents,
employees, employers and developers . . . ." (Emphasis
added.)

     Some decisions of the ITA may have a foreseeable,
material financial effect on the employers of these members,
distinguishable from their effect on the public generally.
Assuming that these decisions come under the conflict of
interest provisions of the Act (Gov. Code Section 87100, et
seq.), the members may have disqualification duties vis-à-vis
these decisions. However, Sections 18703(c) and (d) of 2
California Code of Regulations allow public officials to
participate in agency decisions affecting a specific
industry, trade or profession if the official was
specifically appointed to the agency as a representative of
that industry, trade or profession. (See, Consumers Union v.
Cal. Milk Producers Advisory Bd. (1978) 82 Cal.App.3d 433.)

     Does Section 18703 apply to members of the ITA? In
other words, may a member of the ITA participate in a
decision that will have a foreseeable, material financial
effect on his or her employer, because the member's employer
is an industry, trade or profession which constitutes "a
significant segment of the public," thus triggering the
"public generally" exception to Section 87100? Although the
City Code does not refer expressly to the exception in
Section 18703, the City Council clearly was interested in
having the views of local businesses expressed on the ITA;
requiring members to disqualify themselves from decisions
affecting their employees would vitiate the whole purpose for
having local organizations nominate members.

3.    Does the Proposed Ordinance Affect a "Significant
Segment of the Public"?

     The proposed ordinance which the ITA Board is now
considering will, if enacted by the City Council, impose
certain reporting, administrative and facilities-improvement
requirements on (1) companies employing 25 or more persons in
Irvine, (2) owners of buildings or complexes that meet or
exceed a certain gross square footage threshold (depending on
use) and (3) developers of new non-residential projects.
Companies employing 25 or more persons constitute 775 of the

RUTAN & TUCKER
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS


John Wallace, Esq.
August 12, 1991
Page -4-


7,469 employers in Irvine, or 10.4%. Those 775 companies
employ 74% of the workers in Irvine. The number of buildings
or complexes subject to the ordinance is estimated to be 130.
(It is possible the proposed ordinance may be modified to
affect employers of 50 or more, rather than 25 or more.
There are some 406 such employers in Irvine (5.4% of all
employers) who employ 61.9% of all employees in Irvine.)

Given these numbers, does the proposed ordinance affect
a "significant segment of the public" such that the "public
generally" exception would apply to any action by the ITA
with regard to this proposed ordinance?

I look forward to hearing from you soon with respect to
the above-referenced matter.

Very truly yours,

RUTAN & TUCKER

[signature]

John L. Fellows III
City Attorney
City of Irvine

JLF:in
Enclosure
cc: Stan Wolcott, Esq.
Darwin Kinghorn, Esq.


7/318/048170-0187/001

embedding.qa_text: The letter is from Rutan & Tucker, Attorneys at Law, and it is dated August 12, 1991. The firm lists its partners and staff members on the left and right sides of the page. The letter is addressed to John Wallace, Esq., Fair Political Practices Commission, Legal Division, 428 J Street, Suite 800, P.O. Box 807, Sacramento, CA 95804-0807. The subject of the letter is a request for formal written advice on behalf of the Irvine Transportation Authority (ITA). The City Attorney for the City of Irvine is seeking advice regarding whether the ITA has "decision-making authority" and whether it can recommend approval to the City Council of an ordinance regarding traffic regulation in Irvine. The letter begins with an introduction and then poses the first question: 1. Does the ITA Have "Decision-Making Authority"? The ITA was established in May 1989 to advise the City Council on traffic issues. Section VI.F-501 et seq., copy enclosed.) It is comprised of 11 members, including the 5 City Councilmembers and 6 other members appointed by the City Council. (Irvine Code, Section VI.F-522.) The City Code specifically states that the ITA does not itself have the power to implement any city policies or procedures; rather, the ITA makes recommendations to City Council, which the Council may accept or reject. In order for an ITA decision to become operative, it must be adopted by a City Council resolution or ordinance. (Sections VI.F-533 and 534.) The ITA meets only once a month. It has not yet made a recommendation to the City Council regarding the proposed ordinance. Do the members of the ITA have disqualification and disclosure duties under the Political Reform Act? I believe that the answer turns on whether the ITA is a "board or commission with decision-making authority." (2 Cal. Code of Regs., Section 18700(a)(2).) Because the ITA does not make, compel or prevent final governmental decisions, and because the City Council historically has not approved ITA's recommendations without significant modification, I have tentatively concluded that the ITA does not meet the regulatory definition. Do you agree? If the ITA is deemed not to be a "board or commission with decision-making authority," must its members disqualify themselves from decisions that would affect their financial interests? Must they file Statements of Economic Interests? 2. Are ITA Members Covered by Regulation 18703? Five members of the ITA are nominated by various local organizations (the University of California at Irvine, the Industrial League of Orange County, the Irvine Chamber of Commerce, the Irvine Business Complex Consortium and the Building Industry of Southern California). Within this group of five members, the four members nominated by trade associations typically do not work directly for the association; rather, they are employed by a member company, e.g., the member nominated by the Building Industry Association works for a local builder, not the Association. The membership of industry representatives on the ITA arises from the City Council's desire to have input from all segments of the Irvine community affected by traffic
